text: Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Nonlinear Pricing Complexity and Consumer Behavior (Exploratory) evidence from a uniquely frustrating experiment Joshua Deutschmann, Jeffrey D. Michler, Emilia Tjernström Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström
Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Motivation: firm perspective Nonlinear pricing mechanisms can increase profits over linear prices.

Long menus of costs 2k −1 for k goods Assumes perfect knowledge of demand space Assumes consumers do not make mistakes Real world examples Cable TV providers Digital media Tickets to Broadway plays Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström
Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Motivation: behavioral perspective Nonlinear mechanisms can benefit consumers by offering “tailored” choices.

People make mistakes Assumes no search costs Assumes no stress or cognitive costs Real world examples Value perception & bundling Gym memberships Bandwidth & deliberation Costs ) Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions The known unknown Fundamental tension in nonlinear pricing: More options may ↑consumer surplus but also ↑cognitive costs, ↑probability of mistakes, ↑firm uncertainty regarding demand No closed-form solutions Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions The known unknown Fundamental tension in nonlinear pricing: More options may ↑consumer surplus but also ↑cognitive costs, ↑probability of mistakes, ↑firm uncertainty regarding demand No closed-form solutions How do real people respond to complex nonlinear prices?
Reduce performance Increase drop-out Increase response time Welfare How do consumer errors affect optimal firm behavior?
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Research questions 1 How does price complexity affect consumer choice & welfare? 2 How do price shocks / search difficulty interact with complexity in this context? 3 Do these effects result in changes to optimal firm behavior?
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions What we do Simulate optimal prices Run a lab experiment to measure choice behavior & potential errors how much do participants deviate from optimal? do participants do worse with complex prices? does complexity cause participants to drop out of the market?
Coming: update price simulation with participant behavior how does participant behavior affect profits?
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions What we do & what we find Simulate optimal prices Run a lab experiment to measure choice behavior & potential errors how much do participants deviate from optimal?
Quite a bit! do participants do worse with complex prices?
Yep. does complexity cause participants to drop out of the market?
Coming: update price simulation with participant behavior how does participant behavior affect profits?
Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Generalized setup Monopolist firm selling N goods Consumer valuations: Vi = [Vi1,., ViN] Each valuation drawn from uniform distribution Unit demand for each good Consumers maximize surplus: V ′ i Dj −pj Dj K 1 binary vector indicating goods in bundle j pj price of bundle j Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Pricing strategies Component Pricing (CP) Set price for each good No bundle discount offered k prices Pure Bundling (PB) Set price for bundle No separate sale 1 price Mixed Bundling (MB) Set price for each good Set price for each combination 2k −1 prices Bundle Sized Pricing (BSP) Set price for a single good Set price for each bundle size k prices Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Experimental design Random reservation values for 6 goods Constant for each participant throughout experiment 6 periods of 6 rounds each In each round, randomly draw: Pricing scheme (CP, PB, MB, BSP) Number of goods (2-5) Type of goods (color) Prices simulated-optimal Interface shows cost and surplus of current selection(s) Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Treatments Price Shock: After randomly-selected rounds, extra ‘bonus” round Treatment group: fix scheme, prices ↑40% Control group: fix scheme, redraw basket Information Costs: Two treatment groups: early and late Small cost to reveal surplus of current selection Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Pre-analysis plan Several clear primary hypotheses, which we pre-register Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Pre-analysis plan Several clear primary hypotheses, which we pre-register Many low-weight secondary hypotheses It can cost more in power to pre-register a hypothesis if either of these are sufficiently small: the importance of the hypothesis, uh the prior probability you assign to H0 being false, ph Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Split sample intuition Imagine a main hypothesis, with power 0. (in full data set) Consider a 2nd hypothesis, same power but ph is only 0.
If we correct for multiple hypothesis testing, then power on both is now 0.
So we sacrifice 9% power on main hyp, and gained only 7% power on second Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions “Hybrid” pre-analysis plan Follow “hybrid” split-sample strategy pre-register main hypotheses, test them in full sample use 35% exploratory sample (N=246) to try out secondary update pre-registration with those secondary hypotheses that pass slightly more lenient bar Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström

Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions Boundedly rational consumers?

Consumers clearly make mistakes but what type of mistakes? 1 Not mistakes, failure to fully account for search costs Consumers are rational; firms just needs to account for this 2 Overconfident or biased mis-weighting of options Leads to systematic distortions in demand that firms can exploit by distorting price 3 Fail to choose the best price due to sub-optimal search, confusion comparing bundles, or excessive inertia Leads to noise in demand that is uncorrelated across consumers and may be hard for firms to fully adjust Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström
Introduction Theoretical environment Experimental design Exploratory results Conclusions More work to do!
Characterize types of non-optimal behavior Re-simulate optimal firm behavior based on revealed demand in particular, do consumer errors change which is the profit-maximizing mechanism?

Click behavior during games Effect of feedback between periods Interaction between response time, performance, and drop-out Nonlinear pricing complexity & consumer behavior Deutschmann, Michler, Tjernström
